reasoning: Two co-requisite gates fully block unauthenticated and low-privilege exploitation. Gate 1: capability check. _import() calls can_import() (line 246 of class-cei-core.php), which evaluates current_user_can('install_plugins'). In a default WordPress install this capability belongs exclusively to the Administrator role. Gate 2: nonce. wp_verify_nonce($_REQUEST['cei-import'], 'cei-importing') is checked at line 251 before any file processing occurs. The 'cei-importing' nonce is created ONLY inside the includes/control.php template block that is itself wrapped in 'if (CEI_Core::can_import())' (line 15), meaning the nonce HTML is only ever rendered to users who already satisfy Gate 1. An unauthenticated user receives no nonce; a subscriber, contributor, author, or editor receives no nonce because the can_import() block renders only to install_plugins holders. There is no alternative code path that emits the nonce (the only other nonce in the plugin is the export nonce 'cei-exporting', localized at line 104 of class-cei-core.php and unrelated to import). WordPress wp_create_nonce values are cryptographically keyed to the user session and action string and cannot be guessed. Therefore an attacker who lacks install_plugins cannot obtain a valid nonce and cannot reach line 418 (download_url call). The SSRF is real code but is constrained entirely to Administrator-level users, making it an informational/low finding in a default WordPress install where administrators are already trusted.
